title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
This tutorial

This tutorial is more a discussion and hand-on looking at your projects.
I have your repositories cloned and we will look at them together.
So, these slides are more a sketch of the topics we will discuss and are not self-contained slides.
To follow asynchronously it is best to rewatch the video recorded on Teams.
Ask questions!

Note:

At the end there is a self-paced tutorial for retrieving data using SQL queries on Google Cloud BigQuery. I will not go through this today. It is optional but I recommend to do it, because it is a good example of how to approach retrieval problems with limited knowledge using the right mix of searching, learning, trial and error analysis, and endurance.

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Version control

What problems did you have with git and GitHub?
Let us discuss a few points

Use the Source Control tab in Positron
It is always related to using git on the command line!
Is git doing live updates/synching of the repository? No!
Do all your local files have to be in the repository? No!
What is a commit? What is a push? What is a pull? What is a merge? What is a conflict?
What happens if you edit WORKPLAN.md on GitHub and then pull? The local file will be overwritten!

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Collaboration with Colleages

How do you collaborate in your team?

Do you have a weekly meeting?
Do you divide who works on what files when? It is good to have a plan to avoid too many sources for conflict.
Please maintain a rough work plan in WORKPLAN.md with personalized tasks.
Please stick to the reporting requirements explained in the syllabus!

Please do not overdo reporting and project management. Doing the Data Science is the most important part!

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Collaboration with AI tools

Do you collaborate with AI tools for coding?
Coding is a very common use case for AI tools.
I expose you to some ways. I encourage to try them.
Warning! AI tools can deliver impressive results, especially on standard things, but …

they can stop you from learning and understanding the code and the structure of programming.

Use AI also to have code explained! Read the explanations and ask back if you do not know concepts.

they can also be wrong. They can get stuck and you need to help. See Fun. If you do not understand anything you can not help!

It is a known fact that vibe coding with AI may feel like much faster but helping and understanding with all the problems can lead to effectively similar times as doing it mostly yourself.

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
section: 3 ways to collaborate with AI tools
text:
3 ways to collaborate with AI tools

Use chat interfaces like https://chatgpt.com/, https://claude.ai, https://gemini.google.com, …

Ask for code snippets, explanations, and so on.
You can paste in a blunt error message and often it gives good advice.
Check the Chat tab for the Positron Assistant!


Use code suggestion tools in the IDE. For example GitHub Copilot in Positron.

Students and teachers at universities get a free plan.
Gives you suggestions for the next lines of code.
Warning! Suggestions can be bad this can get annoying. Know the switch off!
Copilot suggestion can also overrule Positrons code completion. You may recieve wrong path suggestions by AI instead of the correct ones by Positron.

Agentic AI tools like Claude Code (Command Line tool solving larger codig tasks).

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Reproducibility / Open Science
We are working on GitHub repositories which can be public.
This is a good step towards open science.

You can share your code and data with the world.

Warning! Not all data should be shared! It may even not be allowed.

Code delivery is a good step towards reproducibility.

Reproducibility means that other people can run your code and get the same results.
But it is not always enough. You also need to document how to run the code and which packages and versions are needed, at least a bit.

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Data “cleaning”

I do not like the term “cleaning”. It is more about understanding the data and making it usable for your analysis. Lets call it data wrangling or data preparation.
It is often the most time-consuming part of a data science project!
For most project: Work on a data preparation script in a coordinated way. The scipt

imports the raw data
produces data frames you need for analysis
writes these out to import them in your analysis and reporting files.

Important skill to learn: Which operation should go into the data preparation script and which should be done in the analysis script?
The scripts leading from raw data to the final report are sometimes called the data pipeline.
What to do with missing data? It depends! Find the appropriate way to deal with them for each of your specific purposes. Want to impute data? My advice is: Do not.

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Machine Learning is very broad

Machine Learning is a very broad field.
It is always about an algorithm that learns from data in a certain way. Often this is about fixing weights.
There are machine learning algorithms for classification, regression, clustering, dimensionality reduction, and so on.
Machine learning is also the backbone of generative AI tools.
For your project you will probably work on machine learning for more classical prediction problems for classification or regression.

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Prediction models

You can always compare the performance of your machine learning model to a simple baseline model.

For the regresssion problem (prediciting a numerical value), a simple baseline is the linear model
For the classification problem (predicting a category), a simple baseline is the logistic regression.

If your model does not perform better than the baseline, you have not done anything wrong. You have just not found a better model.
If your model performs better than the baseline, you have done something right. You have found a better model. It may be better in predicting the data, but it may not be better in understanding the data.

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Doing machine learning

A lot of computer science machine learning is about finding better learning algorithms. However for data science projects it is often more important to find better features.
For data science most work goes into

feature engineering and data preparation. The machine learning part is often just a small part of the project.
Validation. You need to make sure that your model generalizes to new data and does not just memorize the training data. Cross-validation is a common technique for this.

For reporting of machine learning results, it is important to

Explain the prediction problem first!
Understand the particular challenges for your type of problem. (For example: class imbalance for classification problems.)
Have a baseline model for comparison.
Report the performance of your model on a test set that was not used for training.
Understand the performance measures you use and report them correctly.

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Google Cloud BigQuery Public Datasets

We use the Google Cloud Platform which offers several cloud computing services based on Google’s infrastructure.
We use the service BigQuery which is the data warehouse of Google Cloud.
There is a sandbox mode which does not require anymore than a Google Account. (There is even no Free Trial registration or Credit Card Detail necessary!) https://console.cloud.google.com/bigquery

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Google Cloud BigQuery Sandbox
There is a sandbox mode which does not require anymore than a Google Account. No Free Trial registration or Credit Card Details necessary.

Go to https://console.cloud.google.com/bigquery. If necessary, log in with a Google Account.
Click on “Create Project”. Leaving the default specification. Create the project.
Go to the “SQL workspace” (if you are not already there)
In the “Explorer” click “Add Data”, select “Public Datasets”, search for “usa names”, click on the data set “USA Names”, and then “View Dataset”.
The bigquery-public-data should appear in the Explorer.
Use pressing the triangle to navigate the hierarchy in the Explorer. Go to the dataset usa_names and then click on its table usa_1910_2013. To the right should appear a tab with information about the table.

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text: USA Names dataset in BigQuery

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Input: Databases and SQL

Structured Query Language (SQL) is a language to query relational databases.
A relational database

presents data to users in the form of a collection of tables with rows and columns, and
provides relational operators to manipulate the data in tabular form.

SQL is somehow a standard but may come in different flavors
Some call it “S,Q,L”, other “SEQUEL”

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Input: Database = data frame?

Tables in a database are indeed essentially the same as data frames in R, or python-pandas.

What is the different use cases?

SQL databases are

for storing and querying large amounts of data
often being access through a network
often so large to be distributed over several severs

The data frames in our data science tools are for smaller datasets to manipulate and analyze then within a programming environment and typically in memory.

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
section: Let’s do a SQL query on USA Names!
text:
Let’s do a SQL query on USA Names!
On the BigQuery interface The tab for the table usa_1910_2013 shows

The table Schema which shows names and data types of the columns.
What are the columns in USA Names?
Some Details about the data in the table: Metadate and data about Storage
A preview of the first rows of the table


Select “Query” –> “In split tab” to open a new tab for our query.
We want to extract the numbers of babies with the name “Kevin” for each year in the whole USA.
Copy this query

SELECT year, sum(number) AS num
FROM `bigquery-public-data.usa_names.usa_1910_2013`
WHERE name = "Kevin"
GROUP BY year

Click “Run” and explore the results.

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Explanations
SELECT year, sum(number) AS num
FROM `bigquery-public-data.usa_names.usa_1910_2013`
WHERE name = "Kevin"
GROUP BY year

Line 1 specifies what columns we want to have. It also specifies that we compute a new variable num which is the sum of number. To that end, we need some GROUP BY which comes later in Line 4.
Line 2 specifies the data table. Note the dot-notation with the database collection bigquery-public-data, the database usa_names, and the table usa_1910_2013
Line 3 specifies a filter
The line breaks are not relevant for the execution.

The UPPERCASE of the syntax words is not necessary and only a convention.

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Query from R
Now, let’s do the same query from R using a copy of the script Tutorials/CollectingData_YOURNAME.R.

Load the package bigrquery
Execute the bq_auth() command (check the text in the comments)
Use this code

billing <- "blabla-380021"  # This must be your project ID! 
sql <- "SELECT year, sum(number
FROM `bigquery-public-data.usa_names.usa_1910_2013`
WHERE name = 'Kevin'
GROUP BY year"
tb <- bq_project_query(billing, sql)
data <- bq_table_download(tb, n_max = 100)
Important: Replace the billing string with your project ID. Find it clicking here

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Plot the data in R
Now we can look at the evolution of the popularity of the name “Kevin” with
data |> ggplot(aes(year, num, color = name)) + geom_line()

Next challenge: Extract also the data for the name “Martin”. Hint: You need to add “Martin” (comma-separated) to the SELECT and add a condition with OR at the end of the WHERE. You also need to add name to the GROUP BY.

title: Tutorial: Collaboration, Data, Machine Learning, Reporting
text:
Learing SQL
Suppose we have the data frame usa_names in R or python. Then the same data wrangling operations are quite similar.


SQL
SELECT year, sum(number) AS num
FROM `bigquery-public-data.usa_names.usa_1910_2013`
WHERE name = "Kevin"
GROUP BY year

R dplyr
usa_names |> 
filter(name == "Kevin") |> 
select(year, number) |> 
group_by(year) |> 
summarize(num = sum(number))

py pandas
usa_names.loc[usa_names['name'] == 'Kevin', 
['year', 'number']].\
groupby('year').\
agg(num=('number', 'sum'))

There are libraries for linking python or R to SQL databases

Use these when your data is in a database anyway

It may even make sense to make some computations in a SQL query (often much faster!)
SQL skills are useful (often must-have) for data scientists! Whenever there is an opportunity to learn a bit of SQL, take it!
Basics are not too complicated. AI tools can help to translate!

title: Presentation Guide
text:
Each Team presents 3 times. See the Schedule.
These are the guidelines for the presentations
Duration: A schedule will be provided.
Discussion: After each presentation, actively contribute to the discussion challenging and inspiring the other project teams.

title: Presentation Guide
section: 1 💡💭 Proposal Presentation: You present the project you want to do.
text:
1 💡💭 Proposal Presentation: You present the project you want to do.
Goals: Show what your project shall be about, what data and literature you depart from, and a feasible way how to work towards it.
Content: (In brackets an average expectation how much attention to spend on the points. Not a strict rule!)

Explain what main questions or problems your project wants to address. (40%)
Refer to core materials you want to use and how much you have worked into: (20%)

Data: Which data? Have you put it in place already? Can you import it? Have you started to explore it? Aim to show feasibility by doing first steps, runing your idea in a minimal setting, do a first test, …!
Literature and web resources: Which papers, documentations, websites are essential for your project?
Is something essential missing? What additional material would you like to find?

Explain what methods you want to use: (20%)

Do you know which methods can be used to answer your questions or solve your problems?
Which ones will you try first?
How much are you into the method yet?
What can be an interesting learning topic for the whole course?

Show a workplan: What are the most important things to do next? (20%)

Which material to study first with what focus?
How to setup your data and analysis pipeline?
Provide sensible task list
List who is responsible for what


Format: Informal. Show your workplan in your project repository. You can provide some informal slides to show some of your materials or present the materials directly.
Note: First Team Member Contributions report is due.

title: Presentation Guide
text:
2 🚧 Progress Presentation
Goal: Show your progress! First what worked and is done, then what is still open, what confused you, and finally your updated workplan to finish the project. Give some insights on learning which can be of interest for the whole course.
Content:

Questions and problem refined (20%)
Core materials with core essentials extracted: Data and Literature (20%)
Methods: What did you try, what works, what are the challenges? (40%)
Updated workplan (20%)

Important: With each project team we will specify a learning focus which is interesting for the whole course. Share some insights with the class on that topic. For example: What is it? For what it is used? What is easier what is harder to learn than expected?
Format: Mostly Informal is fine. Provide indroductory slides for questions and problems your project is about. For intermeidate results it is enough to show materials, code-files, or output from your repository directly.
Note: Second Team Member Contributions report is due.

title: Presentation Guide
text:
3 👩‍🏫👨‍🏫 Final Presentations
Goal: Show your results! What is the main achievement. Critically reflect.
Format: Do a presentation with slides guiding through the project’s results.
Note: Third and final Team Member Contributions report is due.

title: Tutorial: Writing Text with LLM
text:
Text generating AI Tool

Using text generating AI tools has become a reality and is used for

learning,
translating,
coding,
receiving advice,
leisure, and

for writing and revising text for us.

The backbone of the generative AI tools ar large language models which generate text

title: Tutorial: Writing Text with LLM
text:
Use as a student

Help to solve assessment and other tasks ☑️
👍 Especially for coding, but understand and scrutinize output!
⚠️ Don’t forget the learning!
Learning by asking for explanations ✅
Adjust the detail level 📑 and specificity 🔎 with follow up prompts.
⚠️ Don’t stop at the chat, get productive 🧑‍💻, find authorative sources and continue 📚.
Generate ideas for your own project (☑️)
⚠️ Often generic unspecific ideas.
🧑‍💻 quickly start to get productive.
Write text for your own project reports ((☑️))
⚠️ Often generic unspecific text.
✏️ You have to write the core research questions, arguments, and results yourself!

title: Tutorial: Writing Text with LLM
text:
Prompting workflow
Text development works with chatting via a chain of prompts

You write what you want
Write an outline for data science project about cricket.
You ask for refinement, reshaping, and restructuring on further prompts
Write an abstract of about 200 words on it.
Continue until you are satisfied. Copy, edit and finalize yourself.

title: Tutorial: Writing Text with LLM
section: How LLMs work: Autocomplete on Overdrive
text:
How LLMs work: Autocomplete on Overdrive
Activity
Follow The Bullshit Machines
Chapter 1: Autocomplete on Overdrive
Questions:

Does the same prompt always lead to the same outcome?
(From The Bullshit Machines) Why do you think a program that simply tries to predict the most likely next word in a sequence would be good at holding conversations?

title: Tutorial: Writing Text with LLM
section: How LLMs work: Autocomplete on Overdrive
text:
How LLMs work: Autocomplete on Overdrive
Activity
Why do some call LLM Bullshit Machines?
Follow The Bullshit Machines
Chapter 2: The Nature of Bullshit
Questions:

(From The Bullshit Machines) People are perfectly good at producing bullshit without AI assistance—but with AI, people can produce more bullshit, faster. Who might find that useful? How?

title: Tutorial: Writing Text with LLM
text:
The instructors perspective
The instructor

has to read a lot
must read all student reports with an equal grading mindset
should provide some constructive feedback


Problem
AI generated text increases the amount of text which reads (superficially) well but it can become even harder to find the own contribution. It may read a bit like bullshit. Often it is very generic.

title: Tutorial: Writing Text with LLM
section: AI may be used also by the instructor!
text:
AI may be used also by the instructor!

Summarize student contributions
Write Feedback

(I haven’t done so far ✌🤞️.) However, we want to avoid this

title: Tutorial: Writing Text with LLM
section: Is using text generating AI plagiarism?
text:
Is using text generating AI plagiarism?
Activity
Two (or more) groups to answer the same questions:

What is plagiarism?
Is using AI to write text a form of plagiarism? Why? Why not?

Group 1 Read Constructor University’s Code of Academic Conduct
Group 2-X Ask AI!

title: Tutorial: Writing Text with LLM
text:
Data Science Project Abstract Writing
Activity
Let us try different AI Tools: OpenAI’s ChatGPT, Antrophic’s Claude, Mistral AI’s Le Chat, Google’s Gemini, …
Group 1-X Two on ChatGPT, more with other tools
Use this prompts:

You write what you want
Write an outline for data science project about cricket.
You ask for refinement, reshaping, and restructuring on further prompts
Write an abstract of about 150 words on it.

Discussion: Read out the texts and we discuss its quality from the educated reader’s and instructor’s perspective.

title: Tutorial: Writing Text with LLM
section: Final Activity: Abstract for your project
text:
Final Activity: Abstract for your project
Activity
In your group:
Create a project abstract of about 150 words for your project with as many prompts as you want.
(10-15 minutes)
Discussion: Read your abstracts and we discuss its quality from the educated reader’s and instructor’s perspective.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
section: Communicating and Reporting in the Data Science Process
text:
Communicating and Reporting in the Data Science Process
From R for Data Science:

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Sort our reporting tools



quarto (.qmd)
html
git
pdf
GitHub
json

notebook (.ipynb)
Google Docs, Spreadsheets, Slides

\(\LaTeX\)
Positron
markdown (.md)
pandoc

What are the different categories in this list?

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Tools sorted


File Formats

html
pdf
json
qmd
ipynb
docx, pptx, xlsx


Commandline tools

quarto

Web services

GitHub

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Open-source, freeware, proprietary

Open-source software: Has source code that anyone can inspect, modify, and enhance.

Examples: quarto, git, pandoc, R, python

Proprietary software is software that is owned by an individual or a company and has restrictions on its use, modification, and distribution. It typically requires a license which costs money.

Examples: Microsoft Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Adobe Photoshop

Freeware is software that is available for use at no cost, but it may not necessarily be open-source but can be propriatary.

Examples: Virtual Studio Code, Positron



Common special case:

Software is open-source but developers and maintainers are tied to a company which builds a business around it.

Example: Virtual Studio Code is built on the open-source codebase “Code - OSS” and this is developed and maintained by Microsoft.

So, others can build on and maybe even contribute to
It is available for free, but it is not open-source software. The open-source version of Visual Studio Code is available as “Code - OSS” and can be built from source code, but it does not include some of the proprietary features that are included in the official Visual Studio Code distribution.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Web services
Examples: GitHub, Google Docs, Google Spreadsheets, Google Slides, cloud versions of Microsoft Word, Excel, PowerPoint

Some web service are open-source, but the biggest value of webservice for the consumer is that it can be used to a large extent in the browser without installing software.
Many webservices provide a basic version of their service for free, but they also offer premium features that require a paid subscription.


Paradoxical story:

GitHub is fully proprietary. However, it is built on the open-source software Git.
Moreover, the largest share of open-source software projects are hosted on GitHub (also we do)

BUT, there are open souce alternatives like GitLab and Bitbucket.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
XML, JSON, YAML

XML is a general markup language generalized from HTML developed for structuring webpages.
JSON is a lightweight data-interchange format. It is easier for humans to read and write. It is easy for machines to parse and generate. It has been developed for data storage and transmission, especially in web applications using JavaScript.
YAML is an even more human-readable format that is often used for configuration files and in applications where data is being stored or transmitted. It is designed to be easy to read and write, and it uses indentation to indicate structure.

All are called serialization formats, because they are used to serialize data structures into a format that can be easily stored or transmitted and then deserialized back into the original data structure in memory when needed.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
section: For data science we want data frames
text:
For data science we want data frames
For the representation as a dataframe we have to make design choices:


Two tables
With name as key:



name
age




Alice
30


Bob
25






name
hobby




Alice
reading


Alice
cycling


Bob
gaming




One table
With redundant information:



name
age
hobby




Alice
30
reading


Alice
30
cycling


Bob
25
gaming






The are packages in R and python to read and write all three formats, e.g. yaml in R and PyYAML in python for YAML, jsonlite in R and json in python for JSON, xml2 in R and xml.etree.ElementTree in python for XML.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Markdown and Notebooks
Markdown is a lightweight markup language with plain text formatting syntax

It is often used to format readme files, for writing messages in online discussion forums, and to create rich text using a plain text editor.
Can be rendered to html or other markup languages, e.g. by quarto from a quarto markdown file (qmd).

Notebooks are a way to write and execute code in an interactive environment

Jupyter notebooks are essentially json files that contain code, markdown, and the output of the code.
Important difference to markdown: The code is executed and the output is stored in the notebook file. Quarto runs on a file and renders new files, but does not store the output in the source file.

Check the source file of an ipynb with graphs!

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Quarto

Adapted from the paradigm of literate programming: Computer-readable source code should be interspersed with human-readable explanations.

Original: Write code together with a human-readable documentation report
Quarto: Write a report together with your code of analysis



It is a standalone (cross-language, R-independent) adaptation from Rmarkdown and the R-packages knitr.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
section: Quarto works with R and python1
text:
Quarto works with R and python1



Uses the independent software pandoc “a universal document converter” with markdown as its heart.
You can also render jupyter notebooks (ipynb files) by adding a raw cell with quarto YAML at the beginning.

and other languages

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Quarto for Data Science Projects
Four purposes:

Provide a format to write code, documentation, and other plain text systematically in one file
Provide reproducible data analysis
Provide an environment to update reports easily when new data has arrived
Smoothly move from your working files to publication-ready reports in formats like pdf, docx, html, or a multipage website

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Tutorial today

Make a joint Test Website
Sketch a quarto report for your project

Discuss various aspects of reports

Make a presentation from it
Make a website from it
(optional) Discuss more aspects

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Reports
Types of reports: Article <- Technical Report <- Tutorial <- Homework (many more)



Structure

Header with title, author, and (optional) abstract/executive summary
Main headings: Introduction, Data, Methods, Results, Discussion
Sub-headings: Topic specific
Footnotes
Figures
Tables
Cross-references
Literature references



Code things

Equations (\(\LaTeX\)-style)
Code chunks
Code output
In-text code-output

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Excursion Header: YAML vs. JSON

YAML is a human-readable data serialization standard that can be used in conjunction with all programming languages and is often used to write configuration files.
It is even simpler/more “human-readable” than JSON (which is simpler than XML/HTML)
It uses less brackets and commas but white space (as python)

YAML: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML
Official: https://yaml.org/
Tutorial: https://www.cloudbees.com/blog/yaml-tutorial-everything-you-need-get-started
What’s the difference to JSON? https://aws.amazon.com/compare/the-difference-between-yaml-and-json/
Lets make some specifications in the YAML header for HTML output. https://quarto.org/docs/output-formats/html-basics.html: a table-of-content, code-folding, …

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Tutorial: Authoring and Scholarly Writing
Let us do the following in a quarto markdown file (qmd)


For the following get help at quarto-Guide -> Authoring

YAML with author and abstract
Headlines
An equation
An image
An image created by a code chunk
An in-text code-output of a numerical value


For the following scholarly writing get help at quarto-Guide -> Authoring -> Scholarly Writing

Make additional name information
Make a figure with caption we can refer to
Add a bibliography

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Excursion: Literature Management
Do you use literature management systems?

Google Scholar -> BibTeX
Zotero

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Data Science Workflow
In a normal data science workflow you first:

Do exploratory data analysis (EDA).

This produces many output you need to see to understand the data and progress deeper towards your goal.
You also want to see the code in your output.

Try several versions of different visualizations of your insights.
You end up with a very cluttered file which maybe helpful for you but not for external readers (and in the end not even for you anymore…)

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Workflow: Towards a final report

Clean your code!

Remove unnecessary objects
Structure that it is most readable for others coders (like you in some weeks)

Condense your insights of the EDA to

summarizing text
few nicely formatted tables and informative graphics which are relevant for the reader

Make code and raw output vanish by

removing raw output (or make it a formatted table, or inline code to show code output in markdown text)
hide code or use code folding in html (the user can check the reproducibility and details by folding out the code snippet)

Structure the text: sensible (numbered) headingsines, table of content, cross references to figures, footnotes, citations, appendices …

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Websites, Literature Management
text:
Derivatives of your report

A presentation with revealjs https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/

Duplicate the file, adjust the YAML to revealjs and see how it looks and adjust

A website https://quarto.org/docs/websites/

We need to write a file _quarto.yml to specify the website settings

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Google Cloud BigQuery Public Datasets

We use the Google Cloud Platform which offers several cloud computing services based on Google’s infrastructure.
We use the service BigQuery which is the data warehouse of Google Cloud.
There is a sandbox mode which does not require anymore than a Google Account. (No Free Trial registration or Credit Card Details necessary) https://console.cloud.google.com/bigquery

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Google Cloud BigQuery Sandbox
There is a sandbox mode which does not require anymore than a Google Account. No Free Trial registration or Credit Card Details necessary.

Go to https://console.cloud.google.com/bigquery. If necessary, log in with a Google Account.
Click on “Create Project”. Leaving the default specification. Create the project.
Go to the “SQL workspace” (if you are not already there)
In the “Explorer” click “Add Data”, select “Public Datasets”, search for “usa names”, click on the data set “USA Names”, and then “View Dataset”.
The bigquery-public-data should appear in the Explorer.
Use pressing the triangle to navigate the hierarchy in the Explorer. Go to the dataset usa_names and then click on its table usa_1910_2013. To the right should appear a tab with information about the table.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text: USA Names dataset in BigQuery

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Input: Databases and SQL

Structured Query Language (SQL) is a language to query relational databases.
A relational database

presents data to users in the form of a collection of tables with rows and columns, and
provides relational operators to manipulate the data in tabular form.

SQL is somehow a standard but may come in different flavors
Some call it “S,Q,L”, other “SEQUEL”

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Input: Database = data frame?

Tables in a database are indeed essentially the same as data frames in R, or python-pandas.

What is the different use cases?

SQL databases are

for storing and querying large amounts of data
often being access through a network
often so large to be distributed over several severs

The data frames in our data science tools are for smaller datasets to manipulate and analyze then within a programming environment and typically in memory.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
section: Let’s do a SQL query on USA Names!
text:
Let’s do a SQL query on USA Names!
On the BigQuery interface The tab for the table usa_1910_2013 shows

The table Schema which shows names and data types of the columns.
What are the columns in USA Names?
Some Details about the data in the table: Metadate and data about Storage
A preview of the first rows of the table


Select “Query” –> “In split tab” to open a new tab for our query.
We want to extract the numbers of babies with the name “Kevin” for each year in the whole USA.
Copy this query

SELECT year, sum(number) AS num
FROM `bigquery-public-data.usa_names.usa_1910_2013`
WHERE name = "Kevin"
GROUP BY year

Click “Run” and explore the results.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Explanations
SELECT year, sum(number) AS num
FROM `bigquery-public-data.usa_names.usa_1910_2013`
WHERE name = "Kevin"
GROUP BY year

Line 1 specifies what columns we want to have. It also specifies that we compute a new variable num which is the sum of number. To that end, we need some GROUP BY which comes later in Line 4.
Line 2 specifies the data table. Note the dot-notation with the database collection bigquery-public-data, the database usa_names, and the table usa_1910_2013
Line 3 specifies a filter
The line breaks are not relevant for the execution.

The UPPERCASE of the syntax words is not necessary and only a convention.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Query from R
Now, let’s do the same query from R using a copy of the script Tutorials/CollectingData_YOURNAME.R.

Load the package bigrquery
Execute the bq_auth() command (check the text in the comments)
Use this code

billing <- "blabla-380021"  # This must be your project ID! 
sql <- "SELECT year, sum(number
FROM `bigquery-public-data.usa_names.usa_1910_2013`
WHERE name = 'Kevin'
GROUP BY year"
tb <- bq_project_query(billing, sql)
data <- bq_table_download(tb, n_max = 100)
Important: Replace the billing string with your project ID. Find it clicking here

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Plot the data in R
Now we can look at the evolution of the popularity of the name “Kevin” with
data |> ggplot(aes(year, num, color = name)) + geom_line()

Next challenge: Extract also the data for the name “Martin”. Hint: You need to add “Martin” (comma-separated) to the SELECT and add a condition with OR at the end of the WHERE. You also need to add name to the GROUP BY.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Learing SQL
Suppose we have the data frame usa_names in R or python. Then the same data wrangling operations are quite similar.


SQL
SELECT year, sum(number) AS num
FROM `bigquery-public-data.usa_names.usa_1910_2013`
WHERE name = "Kevin"
GROUP BY year

R dplyr
usa_names |> 
filter(name == "Kevin") |> 
select(year, number) |> 
group_by(year) |> 
summarize(num = sum(number))

py pandas
usa_names.loc[usa_names['name'] == 'Kevin', 
['year', 'number']].\
groupby('year').\
agg(num=('number', 'sum'))

There are libraries for linking python or R to SQL databases

Use these when your data is in a database anyway

It may even make sense to make some computations in a SQL query (often much faster!)
SQL skills are useful (often must-have) for data scientists! Whenever there is an opportunity to learn a bit of SQL, take it!
Basics are not too complicated. AI tools can help to translate!

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Input: URL structure

Example: https://www.example.com:8080/path/to/resource?q=search+terms#section-title

Scheme: https://
Hostname: www.example.com
Port: :8080 (optional component)
Path: /path/to/resource (typically an html-file like index.html)
Query String: ?q=search+terms (optional component - it is used to pass additional data to the server, typically in the form of key-value pairs separated by &)
Fragment Identifier: #section-title (this is an optional component - it is used to identify a specific section or location within the resource that the URL points to)

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Input: URL Structure Examples


The graphic is from the section https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/URL#Syntax (Note the fragment)
https://www.google.com/maps?q=Constructor+University&t=k (Note the query, t=k specifies satellite map)

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Input: Hostnames

An essential part of a URL is the hostname. See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hostname. The hostname of this URL is en.wikipedia.org
Hostnames are linked to IP-Addresses through the Domain Name System (DNS).

Check http://uni-bremen.de and http://134.102.22.124

Hostnames (and other things in URLs) are case-insensitive: lowercase = LOWERCASE

Check http://janlo.de and http://JANLO.DE

Hostnames have the top of the hierarchy at the end: subdomain.domain.top-level-domain.

This order is reverse to file-paths!
/root/parent/child/file vs.
child.parent.root/file

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Input: Website description languages

HTML (Hypertext Markup Language): The essential language used for creating web pages and defining the structure and content of a website.

Almost all modern websites also include:

CSS (Cascading Style Sheets): Used for defining the visual style and layout of a website, including colors, fonts, and spacing.
JavaScript: Used to add interactivity and dynamic functionality to web pages, such as animations, form validation, and interactive user interfaces on the client (=user) side.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Input: Website description languages
Often CSS and JavaScript are loaded from external files specified in the html-file.
Exercise in the html “Page Source” find a link to a .css file and open it, find a link to a .js file and open it.
Common tools:

PHP: Server-side scripting language that is used for building dynamic websites and web applications, such as content management systems and e-commerce platforms.
SQL: This language is used for managing and manipulating data stored in a database, which is often used to power dynamic websites.

Example wordpress: When you call a wordpress-site like http://janlo.de/wp/index.php, the webserver of the site uses the PHP script to compile an html-page using data in an SQL-database and sends it back to your browser.
Instead of PHP also other languages/webframeworks can be used like Ruby on Rails or one of the several python webframeworks.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Input: JavaScript Object Notation (JSON)
JSON has emerged as a standard for web data exchange.

It uses attribute-value pairs and arrays
It is used for data exchange of algorithms
It is also human-readable because
It is independent of JavaScript but was derived from it.
It is more flexible than a data frame. Reading it in R or python you need nested lists.
R and python provide packages which helpreading (parts of) JSON data into data frames (if possible)

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Input: Application Programming Interface (API)

JSON is often the output of queries using Application Programming Interfaces (API) (sometimes) provided by Web Services like GitHub.
Note: APIs are not only used for accessing data but also ingesting or modifying data! Here we focus on data access.
The purpose of APIs is to limit the amount of data that needs to be transferred between the client and the server.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
section: Downloading and reading JSON from GitHub
text:
Downloading and reading JSON from GitHub
We can use the GitHub API.
We can get the JSON file from the user janlorenz by: http://api.github.com/users/janlorenz
Let us do the following in R:
githubname = "janlorenz"
download.file(glue("https://api.github.com/users/{githubname}"), destfile = glue("{githubname}.json"))
user <- read_json(glue("{githubname}.json"))
Now you have downloaded the JSON file, and read it into the R object user.
Explore this nested list object!

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
The tbl_json object is nested
For example: The Name of the user is at
user$`..JSON`[[1]]$name
Explanations

user is a list of 2 with the strange item name ..JSON we have to put it in backticks ` to access it with $.
user$`..JSON` is a list of one without named items. Seems quite useless here but maybe helpful when we have more users.
user$`..JSON`[[1]] is a list with all the items available for the user! So we can extract the name.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Extract the following of janlorenz

Look up the API url of the following of janlorenz from the object user
Produce it yourself as glue(https://api.github.com/users/{githubname}/following)
Use the code above to download the JSON of the following and read it into an object following.
We try to make a data frame df from the followers object followers using the tidyjson package. (Test: spread_all)
following$..JSON[[1]] |> spread_all()

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
section: Code for number of following and followers
text:
Code for number of following and followers
download.file(glue("https://api.github.com/users/{githubname}/following"), destfile = glue("{githubname}-following.json"))
download.file(glue("https://api.github.com/users/{githubname}/followers"), destfile = glue("{githubname}-followers.json"))
following <- read_json(glue("{githubname}-following.json"))
following$`..JSON`[[1]]
df_following <- following$`..JSON`[[1]] |> spread_all()
followers <- read_json(glue("{githubname}-followers.json"))
df_followers <- followers$`..JSON`[[1]] |> spread_all()
df_following$login
df_following$login |> length() # Number of following
df_followers$login
df_followers$login |> length()

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Retrieve followers of following …
df_following$login |> 
  walk(\(x) download.file(glue("https://api.github.com/users/{x}/followers"), 
                          destfile = glue("following_{x}.json")))
follower_following_user <- df_following$login |> 
  map(\(x) read_json(glue("following_{x}.json"))$`..JSON`[[1]] |> spread_all())
follower_following_user |> map(\(x) x[["login"]])
follower_following_user |> map_dbl(\(x) x[["login"]] |> length())
Note: The maximum number of retrieved followers seems to be limited 30.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
section: Can I get data from any site with APIs?
text:
Can I get data from any site with APIs?
No.

The data of companies is their value. Although, many webservices use APIs, most of them have them private. For example

you cannot get things from Facebook
Twitter just closed the free research API which was quite popular among researchers
Netflix had an open one but closed it some time ago
reddit and github allow a bit

Even when an API is open, it often requires an API key, a token, you get with a user account. Sometimes you have to provide a reason and commit to not use it for commercial purposes. The company can also monitor how you use their API!
Even when an API is open, usually there are rate limits. After some amount of of usage your are cut of for a while or get banned.
Every API is different. Typically, access is with URLs (but which ones?) and the output is JSON (but how structured?)

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Learning APIs and JSON

Watchout for packages wrapping API usage
Use packages to transform JSON to data frames

JSON is very nested so you need to learn to get used to fiddle around with nested data structures (lists of lists of lists …)

Beware, that APIs are often limited so you need some persistence to find out if something is feasible.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
The Internet Movie Database
IMDb is a very popular site with information about movies and TV shows including a \(1\bigstar - 10\bigstar\) user rating module.
Goal: We want to have the number of all star ratings for a movie in a vector.


IMDb has no public API. So we will try to scrape the data from the Website directly.
The URL structure of IMDb is easy. Each movie has an ID which is part of the website. For example
Oppenheimer: https://www.imdb.com/title/tt15398776/ratings/
Barbie: https://www.imdb.com/title/tt1517268/ratings
This already shows the page with the rating distribution.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Letterboxd
Letterboxd uses 10 half-\(\bigstar\). So, 5\(\bigstar\) is maximal.
Oppenheimer: https://letterboxd.com/film/oppenheimer-2023/
Barbie: https://letterboxd.com/film/barbie/
Goal: We want to have the number of all star ratings for a movie in a vector.

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Steps to retrieve the distributions
Steps:

Load the rvest (“harvest” …) package
Use html_read_live to create a live session to a certain URL (the movie-pages)
Find out how we can extract the relevant html-nodes “Inspect” by right-clicking on the object of interest on the webpage in the browser.
We use the function html_elements and html_text to get the values

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Code for IMDb Oppenheimer
session <- read_html_live("https://www.imdb.com/title/tt15398776/ratings/")
session |> html_elements("tspan")
session |> html_elements("tspan") |> html_text()
session |> html_elements("tspan") |> html_text() |> 
  word(1) |> str_extract("\\d+\\.\\d+") |> as.numeric() |> na.omit() |> rev() |> 
  barplot(main = "IMDb Oppenheimer")

title: Tutorial: Collecting Data
Tutorial: Collecting Data
text:
Learnings

Why do we need the “live” session and not just a downloaded HTML-file?

Nowadays, often the interesting part is loaded via some java-script files and only appears on the website once it is sent to a specific browser!

You need to learn a bit of the structure of HTML and CSS.
Learn to fiddle around with strings is often key!

To that end regular expressions can be magic (see https://xkcd.com/208/)! AI Tools can help you get the one you need.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
section: Communicating and Reporting in the Data Science Process
text:
Communicating and Reporting in the Data Science Process
From R for Data Science:

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
Sort our reporting tools



quarto (.qmd)
html
git
pdf
GitHub
json
Virtual Studio Code
notebook (.ipynb)
Google Docs, Spreadsheets, Slides



PowerPoint
\(\LaTeX\)
Positron
markdown (.md)
pandoc
Word
Excel


What are the different categories in this list?

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
Tools sorted


File Formats

html
pdf
json
qmd
ipynb
docx, pptx, xlsx


Commandline tools

quarto
git
pandoc
\(\LaTeX\)


IDEs / App’s

VSCode
RStudio
Excel
PowerPoint
Word


Web services

GitHub
Google Docs
Google Spreadsheets
Google Slides

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
Open-source, freeware, proprietary

Open-source software: Has source code that anyone can inspect, modify, and enhance.

Examples: quarto, git, pandoc, R, python

Proprietary software is software that is owned by an individual or a company and has restrictions on its use, modification, and distribution. It typically requires a license which costs money.

Examples: Microsoft Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Adobe Photoshop

Freeware is software that is available for use at no cost, but it may not necessarily be open-source but can be propriatary.

Examples: Virtual Studio Code, Positron

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
Common special case

Software is open-source but developers and maintainers are tied to a company which builds a business around it.

Example: Virtual Studio Code is built on the open-source codebase “Code - OSS” and this is developed and maintained by Microsoft.

Others can build on and maybe even contribute to it.
Positron did that (using Code - OSS) and added some data science specific features.
Microsoft did it themselves with Virtual Studio Code

So, Virtual Studio Code and Positron ar both propriatary freeware (for private or educational users). They build a business around business and cloud usage.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
Web services
Examples: GitHub, Google Docs, Google Spreadsheets, Google Slides, cloud versions of Microsoft Word, Excel, PowerPoint

Some web service are open-source, but the biggest value of webservice for the consumer is that it can be used to a large extent in the browser without installing software.
Many webservices provide a basic version of their service for free, but they also offer premium features that require a paid subscription.


Paradoxical story:

GitHub is fully proprietary. However, it is built on the open-source software Git.
Moreover, the largest share of open-source software projects are hosted on GitHub (also we do)

BUT, there are open souce alternatives like GitLab and Bitbucket.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
XML, JSON, YAML

XML is a general markup language generalized from HTML developed for structuring webpages.
JSON is a lightweight data-interchange format. It is easier for humans to read and write. It is easy for machines to parse and generate. It has been developed for data storage and transmission, especially in web applications using JavaScript.
YAML is an even more human-readable format that is often used for configuration files and in applications where data is being stored or transmitted. It is designed to be easy to read and write, and it uses indentation to indicate structure.

All are called serialization formats, because they are used to serialize data structures into a format that can be easily stored or transmitted and then deserialized back into the original data structure in memory when needed.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
section: For data science we want data frames
text:
For data science we want data frames
For the representation as a dataframe we have to make design choices:


Two tables
With name as key:



name
age




Alice
30


Bob
25






name
hobby




Alice
reading


Alice
cycling


Bob
gaming




One table
With redundant information:



name
age
hobby




Alice
30
reading


Alice
30
cycling


Bob
25
gaming






The are packages in R and python to read and write all three formats, e.g. yaml in R and PyYAML in python for YAML, jsonlite in R and json in python for JSON, xml2 in R and xml.etree.ElementTree in python for XML.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
Markdown and Notebooks
Markdown is a lightweight markup language with plain text formatting syntax

It is often used to format readme files, for writing messages in online discussion forums, and to create rich text using a plain text editor.
Can be rendered to html or other markup languages, e.g. by quarto from a quarto markdown file (qmd).

Notebooks are a way to write and execute code in an interactive environment

Jupyter notebooks are essentially JSON files that contain code, markdown, and the output of the code.
Important difference to markdown: The code is executed and the output is stored in the notebook file. Quarto runs on a file and renders new files, but does not store the output in the source file.

Check the source file of an ipynb with graphs!

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
Quarto

Adapted from the paradigm of literate programming: Computer-readable source code should be interspersed with human-readable explanations.

Original: Write code together with a human-readable documentation report
Quarto: Write a report together with your code of analysis



It is a standalone (cross-language, R-independent) adaptation from Rmarkdown and the R-packages knitr.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
section: Quarto works with R and python1
text:
Quarto works with R and python1



Uses the independent software pandoc “a universal document converter” with markdown as its heart.
You can also render jupyter notebooks (ipynb files) by adding a raw cell with quarto YAML at the beginning.

and other languages

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
Quarto for Data Science Projects
Four purposes:

Provide a format to write code, documentation, and other plain text systematically in one file
Provide reproducible data analysis
Provide an environment to update reports easily when new data has arrived
Smoothly move from your working files to publication-ready reports in formats like pdf, docx, html, or a multipage website

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
Tutorial today

Make a one page Website with Github Pages.

You can use this for the FinalProject from Data Science Tools

Make a multipage Website with quarto using .qmd and .ipynb files.

You should use this for the Data Science Lab project report. In particular, when you have independent parts.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
section: One page website with GitHub Pages
text:
One page website with GitHub Pages
Prelminary Note: By doing this tutorial you make your work public on the internet. You are not obliged to do. You can skip to do this here and just learn how to do it.


In your repository on your computer

Rename the one page HTML to index.html
Push the file to GitHub

In your repository on GitHub

Make the repository public (Scroll down to “Danger Zone” in Settings)
Go to “Pages” in the left sidebar and select the branch and folder where your index.html is located (e.g. main and /root if it is in the root of your repository)




After a few minutes, your website should be available at https://<organization-name>.github.io/<repository-name>/

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
Multipage website with quarto
Core idea:

Specify the website and its stucture in a Config File _quarto.yml file.
Write the content of the pages in .qmd files.

All information: https://quarto.org/docs/websites/

You can publish this website on GitHub Pages as well, but you have to render the website first and push the rendered files (in the _site folder) to GitHub or use the more advanced methods using

a branch gh-pages (way the instructor) or
GitHub Actions (requires configuring a whole software setup on GitHu such that rendering runs remotely and automatically on GitHub after each commit).

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
Excursion: Literature Management

You should start to use a reference manager for your literature management.
A de facto standard for reference management is Zotero.

It is open-source, free, and has a large user base.

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
section: Zotero Features to make use of
text:
Zotero Features to make use of

Store and organize your literature (including pdfs and your notes)
Has browser extensions to easily add literature from the web with one click while on the website (knows many journals)
Create bibliographies (collections) in different citation styles
Integrate with word processors and markdown editors (e.g. quarto)
Has a free tier for web storage in sync with your local Zotero
You can collaborate with others in making a shared collections of references
Some people manage to import their Zotero notes in their note-taking apps (like Obsidian)

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
In week 4 and 5
Week 4:
Individual meetings with me. Will be scheduled by me in the regular meeting times.
You can still reshuffle teams but I aim for

Preliminary project check-in by team members.
Creating a repository for each project.


Week 5:

Proposal presentations by teams.
Follow the guidelins on this website.
You also have to prepare some basic workplan and team member roles and tasks in your repository (see Syllabus). A template shall be provided

title: Communicating and Reporting of Data Science Projects, Literature Management
text:
Now

Let us look at the current distribution. In the Ad-hoc group sheet.
Time to coordinate Teams and coordinate in Teams.

title: Data Science Lab
text: This syllabus may change during the course of the semester additions and clarifications. All substantial changes will marked here with date.

title: Data Science Lab
text:

title: Data Science Lab

5 Format and Workload

Work on project (125 hours)

title: Data Science Lab
text:
6 Intended Learning Outcomes
The following 8 points are the Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs) taken from the Handbook.
The bold text below each ILO operationalizes the ILO for assessment of your project submission (see Section 7.3).
By the end of this module, students should be able to

understand and critically evaluate current data science applications, identify new innovative research questions and data science applications
The project communicates the problem, approach, and results effectively and clearly. It also critically evaluates the approach and/or results.
experiment with and simulate data science solutions
The project describes how the presented approach or the results may practically be used in data science application.
write/configure computer programs/tools specific to certain subject areas
The tools used in the project are well chosen.
master relevant data pre/post-processing routines
The project shows and documents the data flow from its origin to the results which can be reproduced.
design and schedule a DS project of medium complexity, including escape options, and keep milestones/timelines
This not an assessment criterion, but is realized through the activities and presentations during the semester.
improve their academic writing skills
The text in the project reports is well structured and well written.
communicate results to a non-expert audience
The text has an introduction or abstract and conclusion which are understandable by a non-expert audience.
design their own digital application
This is not an assessment criterion. The ILO is touched in 2.


6.1 Main Learning Goal
This Data Science Lab shall deepen your skills for your more and more independent data science work based on a self-designed project. That means you become more experienced in

in using the core concepts in data science on your own, for example

exploring data (import, wrangle, visualize)
learning, exploring and applying mathematics and statistics through the data science lens
learning and applying concepts to model and draw conclusions from data (model, infer, predict)

creating and maintaining a digital working environment on your computer to do data science
programming in the data science languages R and/or python, and become able to learn new skills in these independently
improve your skills in documenting and presenting your findings with compelling visualizations, descriptions, interpretations, and narratives

title: Data Science Lab
text:
7 Examination and Assessment
Assessment Type: Project Assessment (see Section 6) Weight: 100%
Scope: All intended learning outcomes of the module (see Section 6).
Completion: to pass this module, the examination has to be passed with at least 45%

7.1 Team work policy

Projects have to be done in teams of students.
Every team member has to contribute substantially to the success of the project.
Teams have to provide three Team Member Contributions reports. The dates are analog to the three presentations. All team members must be listed. Note down for each team member:

full name
role
contributions so far
expected contributions until project end

All three Team Member Contributions reports shall be provided on the same dedicated file TEAM-CONTRIBUTIONS.md in the project repository.
Team Grading: By default project reports are graded collectively such that all team members receive the same grade. However, there are two exceptions:

The instructor reserves the option to change form collective to individual grades when workload and contributions are very unbalanced. Indications for this are the Team Member Contributions report, presence and participation during presentations (proposal, progress, and final), and contributions to the repository. The team members will be informed when individual grading applies.
Teams can opt for partially individual grades. To that end, the report needs identifiable parts made by individual members or subgroups, typically individual pages. In any case, the project needs a collectively written abstract, introduction, and conclusion. The individual grade will than be computed 50% by a team grade (based mainly on abstract, introduction, and conclusion) and 50% by an individual grade. Alignment of the individual work to the overall project is a component in assessment of the individual work.

Teams have to ask for this option.
The structure of the individual contributions has to be discussed with the instructor.





7.2 Project Delivery
The project components must be delivered in a project-repository on GitHub provided by the instructor in the GitHub-Organization CU-S26-MSDSSB-DSCI-01-DataScienceLab.
Mark the finalization of the project with commit message “Finalized project”. It will be cloned after the submission deadline and assessed from the repository content.
If your project requires another way of delivery, get in contact with the instructor before the submission.


7.3 Project Format
Lab projects can have different formats. The main contribution need not be a text. It will be developed during the course.
For proper assessment, the repository should have at least one human-readable document which can be read without considering code. Examples:

A rendered quarto file in html-format guiding through the project. These files can be best viewed on a local machine in a browser (but not from the GitHub-Website in a private repository). However, they can be made publically viewable from the Internet via GitHub-pages with a few clicks later if you want.

Files in GitHub flavored markdown (gfm) like the typical README.md. These have slightly fewer formatting options but can be viewed directly on GitHub. This may be a good solution for code-heavy projects.
Alternative or additional options which may be used with a particular reason: A slide deck in revealjs-html or PDF-file. A paged report as a PDF-file.
In any case: Do not submit your work in Word or PowerPoint format (e.g., docx, pptx) or related formats from Apple or Libre Office. All these formats can be converted to PDF – submit such as a PDF.

The instructor starts assessment with reading the README.md of the project repository, then going to the main description file specified as the main human-readable document. Assessment criteria are written down in Section 6.
The repository should also include all the code used to do data collection and import, data analysis, visualizations, predictions, and whatever else the project is about.


7.4 Project “Length”
Your text should begin with a section called Abstract, Outline, or Executive Summary with between 120 and 500 words.

There is no minimal or maximal number of words! (Some project’s main part maybe code or visualizations.)
There is no minimal or maximal number of lines of code! (Some project’s main part may be the documentation, description, interpretation, and critical reflection.)

There is no minimal or maximal number of pages! (You can count pages only if you deliver a slidedeck, or a PDF.)

Some guidelines are:

A short contribution is good when it is concise and covers all aspects. A long contribution is good when it is well structured (in a hierarchy of importance) and not redundant.
If your human-readable text is much less than 3,000 words: Consider if some parts should be extended. Go in detail over the assessment criteria and check that your document covers all the aspects which need to be assessed, is well readable, clear, and concise for an external reader. If this is the case it is great when you can stay below 3,000 words!
If your human-readable text has more than 6,000 words: Consider to

make it shorter by making it more concise and less redundant
structure it in a core part with less than 3,000 words and appendices with additional information (your main text can refer to appendix sections)
using footnotes for additional information (However, avoid too extensive use of long footnotes. At some point it is better to make an appendix section.)

The assessment goes only along the criteria listed below. A long contribution may receive lower assessments when it communicates less effectively and less to the point. Beware not to deliver generic ChatGPT-like “bla-bla”!
Check and polish your code. Check if the code reproduces your results. Remove unnecessary code, and spend some explanatory comments on interesting non-standard steps. Large parts of unnecessary code can be considered as less effective and less clear communication. However, your code need not be excessively educational with a comment on every line.

title: Data Science Lab
text:
8 Module Policies
There are no formal requirement about attendance and active participation. However, the instructor relies on your engagement in a many-facted way including:

Preparation (looking at readings and material before and after class, being informed about syllabus and course material)
Focus (avoid distraction during in class and self-learning activities)
Presence (listening and responding during group activities)
Asking questions (in class, out of class, online, offline, when you get stuck conclude by writing a question)
Specificity (being as specific as possible when describing your problem or question)
Synthesizing (making connections between concepts from reading and discussion)
Persistence (you don’t need to understand everything immediately, but stay engaged, try again, confusion shows that you pay attention)

title: Data Science Lab
text:
9 Academic Integrity
All involved parties (professors and lecturers, instructors and students) are expected to abide by the word and spirit of the Code of Academic Integrity. Violations of the Code will be brought to the attention of the Academic Integrity Committee.

title: Data Science Lab
text:
10 Artifical Intelligence (AI) Use Policy
This policy covers any generative AI tool, such as ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Elicit, etc. This includes text, code, slides, artwork/graphics/video/audio and other products.
I encourage you to using and exploring AI tools for these purposes:

Learning by dialog with a chatbot. AI chatbots can be helpful to explain you concepts on your desired level and get a feeling about how certain topics are treated. You can ask for an easier or more detailed explanation or focus on certain aspects. Note: The capabilities are limited and you likely receive a lot of false information! Using chatbots should remain a small part of your learning process. Rule of thumb: Spend not more than 25% of the learning time with chatting. There is no way around reading textbooks, reading software documentation, learning and understanding concepts, practicing, searching for help online, asking instructors or fellow students.
Have code snippets written. Tools like GitHub Copilot (free to use for students and teachers) are heavily used and currently change code-writing. They can speed up writing your code. However, they do not deliver always correct solutions. There is no way around understanding yourself what a code is doing! Do not spend endless hours asking for new code with new prompts, spend time understanding a language and the functions and objects you are using! Copilots can be a great help to get a skeleton of code and an idea how your solution might look, they rarely deliver the complete code. Expect that the code does not work, expect that the code seems to works but the results are wrong. You are 100% accountable for the code you produce, with or without the help of a copilot!
Have a draft text snippet written. Data science is also about formulating and describing research questions, describing data, documenting code, interpreting results, and deriving conclusion form the results. This is all verbal text and chatbots are good in writing text which often appears well readable. You can use this to inspire yourself and to polish and improve your texts. However, you are 100% accountable for the text you deliver. You are expected to know what your text is about and to be able to answer questions about what your text means! In text generated purely by a chatbot it is often evident that you do not.

Note: Philosophical and legal questions around the training and use of chatbots and code copilots are controversially contested and re-examined constantly! We encourage to engage with such questions and become aware of arguments and debates.
Note on assessment of your text be it written with or without AI: Text written by chatbots is often very generic and less specific. Often, ChatBots deliver bullshit (as defined by H. Frankfurt) . Large parts of very generic bullshit-like text will be assessed worse than a short more specific text!
If any part of this AI policy is confusing or uncertain, please reach out to us for a conversation before submitting your work.
